retrieved_docs.title: Oral Facial Infection of Dental Origin: A Guide for the Medical Practitioner -- Etiology -- Pulp Involvement
retrieved_docs.content: An apical abscess can further complicate pulp necrosis. Apical abscess, dentoalveolar abscess, and odontogenic abscess are usually used interchangeably to describe the localized collection of pus around the periapex due to the spread of infection of the root canal. [9] Acute or chronic forms of periapical abscess can develop.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Facial Infection of Dental Origin: A Guide for the Medical Practitioner -- Etiology -- Pulp Involvement
retrieved_docs.content: Management of an acute apical abscess involves drainage of the affected tooth, followed by endodontic therapy to eliminate necrotic pulp tissue and restore the tooth's structural integrity. In cases where the tooth cannot be preserved, extraction serves as an appropriate alternative to eliminate the source of infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Facial Infection of Dental Origin: A Guide for the Medical Practitioner -- Etiology -- Pulp Involvement
retrieved_docs.content: The dental pulp can be irreversibly damaged due to persistent inflammation, leading to constant, spontaneous, and acute dental pain that is poorly localized. Irreversible pulpitis is a primary reason patients present to the emergency department. [7] Irreversible pulpitis must be referred to a dentist, as the standard of care is partial or complete pulp removal. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- History and Physical -- Initial Apical Periodontitis
retrieved_docs.content: Activated macrophages release other cytokines that intensify the vascular response and osteoclastic bone resorption. At this point, the acute phase may take different courses: healing or worsening the infection. When root canal therapy is initiated, and the offending microbes are removed from the root canal system, the lesion may resolve, and the periapical tissues will remain unchanged radiographically. [14] Conversely, if the infection is not resolved, it will become chronic, receiving the name of chronic apical periodontitis. [8] These lesions may spread through the bone to other fascial spaces causing abscesses or opening to the exterior, creating a fistula.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Etiology -- Infectious Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: Dental caries are the basis for the majority of dental infections. While dental caries are not life-threatening, their presence can predispose patients to oral cavity infections. Other causes of dental infections include trauma or operative manipulation such as root canal treatment and third molar extractions. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Etiology -- Infectious Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: Infections of dental origin prompt patients to seek emergency treatment mainly due to severe pain or swelling. These include irreversible pulpitis, necrosis, periodontitis, and apical and periodontal abscesses (see Image. Dental Abscess). While periodontal disease does not normally induce pain, a severe necrotizing form of the disease exists, which is characterized by an aggressive infection with extreme gingival pain. [11] Pericoronitis, the infection of the gingival tissue overlying an erupting or partially erupted tooth, and typically a 3rd molar, may also cause localized pain and swelling.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Pathophysiology -- Periapical Abscess
retrieved_docs.content: The apex of a tooth is the area where the nerve enters the tooth through the root tip. Bacterial infections of the pulp can progress and collect in the periapical region, creating an abscess. Initially, this abscess is small and focal to the involved tooth. However, local extension of this infection can lead to serious complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Epidemiology -- Infectious Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: Dental infections make up the majority of hospital admissions from the emergency department for those presenting with dental-related complaints. Most people who visit the emergency department with a dental complaint are discharged. Of the 5% who require admission, an overwhelming majority (85%) have a diagnosis of dental infection with diseases of the pulp and periapical tissue. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Abscess -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Combined periodontic-endodontic abscesses: can be categorized according to the origin of the infection into primary endodontic abscess with secondary periodontal involvement, primary periodontal abscess with secondary endodontic involvement, or a true combined periodontal-endodontic abscess. Correct diagnosis relies on collaborating the history, clinical examination, and pulp testing, and intraoral radiographs. [18] Positive pulp testing suggests periodontal origin, whereas a lack of response suggests endodontic origin. Pericoronal abscess: abscesses from a partially erupted tooth can often mimic a periodontal abscess. Clinicians should look out for vital adjacent teeth with no increased periodontal pocketing. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Severe inflammatory reactions can lead to pulpal cell injury, potentially compromising pulpal vitality and survivability. Although the infection activates an immune response, immune cells and molecules are unable to effectively penetrate dentin to remove pathogens due to the narrow apical foramen of the tooth and the hard enamel surrounding the internal structures. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment aims to remove or significantly reduce the intracanal microbes and prevent re-infection by placing a root canal filling. [17] When endodontic treatment is adequately done, the periapical lesion heals with hard tissue regeneration, which is evident in follow-up radiographs through a reduction in the radiolucency's size. [17]  Apical periodontitis lesions are expected to heal completely within six months to two years, but some may persist. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Facial Infection of Dental Origin: A Guide for the Medical Practitioner -- Etiology -- Pulp Involvement
retrieved_docs.content: An apical abscess generally presents as a localized infection that does not warrant antimicrobial therapy before referral to a dental practitioner. [5] Antibiotic treatment becomes necessary only when clinical signs indicate systemic dissemination of the infection, eg, the presence of fever or cellulitis. [5] In such cases, broad-spectrum antibiotics are preferred, with amoxicillin/clavulanate serving as the most frequently prescribed agent. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Facial Infection of Dental Origin: A Guide for the Medical Practitioner -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Dental infections, though often considered minor, can escalate rapidly and result in serious complications if not promptly recognized and managed. This activity equips clinicians with the knowledge to identify, assess, and manage dental infections that present in medical settings. Participants will gain an understanding of the pathophysiology and clinical manifestations of dental caries, pulpitis, apical abscesses, periodontal disease, and pericoronitis, as well as distinguishing between reversible and irreversible pulpitis, appropriate use of antibiotics, red flag symptoms, and indications for emergency referral—particularly in cases of Ludwig angina or airway compromise.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Abscess -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Dental infections, while relatively straightforward regarding diagnosis and access, can be challenging to manage acutely. Dental abscesses or periapical infections typically arise secondary to dental caries (tooth rot related to poor dental hygiene), trauma, or failed dental root canal treatment. Left untreated, these infections can be extremely painful and pose a significant risk of descending into the deep neck space or ascending to intracranial sinuses. Identifying, treating, and educating patients about a dental abscess will grant symptomatic relief and prevent dangerous complications. This activity explains when this condition should be considered for differential diagnosis, articulates how to evaluate this condition properly, and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in caring for patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Thus, we can describe apical periodontitis as an inflammatory disease due to the dynamic interaction between oral bacterial invaders and the body's defensive mechanism at the tooth apex. [7] Despite the complex host defensive system, the causative organisms are well-entrenched in the intricate and delicate root canal system, which is beyond the reach of the host immune response. [8] Therefore, apical periodontitis is not self-healing, and resolution of the disease can only result through surgical or non-surgical endodontic therapy or removal of the tooth in question. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Emergencies -- Treatment / Management -- Infectious Dental Emergencies
retrieved_docs.content: As with any infection, complete removal of the source is always ideal but not practical in the case of dental infections. Tooth extraction is an option for certain patients but is usually the last resort. Often, other techniques are recommended so patients can maintain their dentition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1448
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain is a clinical symptom that can be associated with تضيق الأبهر (aortic stenosis), a cardiac condition characterized by narrowing of the aortic valve and resulting hemodynamic compromise, as well as with داء المبيضات الفموي (oral thrush), an infection of the oral mucosa caused by Candida species. While تضيق الأبهر represents a direct cardiovascular cause of chest discomfort, داء المبيضات الفموي may reflect systemic or immunologic factors that can also manifest with chest pain, highlighting that nonspecific chest pain may arise from both cardiac and non‑cardiac origins. Recognizing this dual association is important for clinicians to conduct a thorough evaluation and to tailor management strategies appropriately.

This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1860
retrieved_docs.content: Ciprofloxacin, a broad‑spectrum fluoroquinolone antibiotic, may be contraindicated or its use may be avoided in the presence of an infection caused by unicellular organisms (عدوى وحيدات الخلية), underscoring the need to consider the specific type of pathogen when selecting antimicrobial therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي) is clinically associated with nonspecific chest pain and with fatigue (التَّعَب). These findings indicate that patients with oral candidiasis may present not only with typical oral lesions but also with systemic or atypical symptoms such as unexplained chest discomfort and generalized tiredness, underscoring the importance of considering this fungal infection in the differential diagnosis when such nonspecific complaints arise.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2857
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Allowed qualifier” denotes that radiotherapy (“radioterapie”) is recognized as an acceptable therapeutic option for patients with tongue disorders (اِضطِراب اللسان), indicating that the use of radiotherapy is permitted and appropriate in the management of this specific oral condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2768
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain, a symptom that lacks a clearly defined cause, has been reported to be clinically associated with oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي). This relationship suggests that patients who present with unexplained chest discomfort may warrant evaluation for an underlying fungal infection of the oral cavity, particularly when other risk factors for candidiasis are present. Recognizing this association can help clinicians adopt a more comprehensive diagnostic approach and ensure appropriate treatment for both the chest pain and any concurrent oral candidiasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 698
retrieved_docs.content: The choroidea (choroid) is repeatedly identified as the primary anatomic site for a broad spectrum of ocular pathologies. Inflammatory disorders localized to the choroid include disseminated choroiditis, focal choroiditis, generalized disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis, peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis, metastatic disseminated retinitis‑retinochoroiditis, as well as combined entities such as التهاب المشيمية (choroiditis), التهاب المشيمية والشبكية (choroiditis‑retinitis), and the unspecified focal variant التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد. Post‑inflammatory and post‑traumatic sequelae—post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scar, post‑inflammatory choroid scar, and post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar—are also situated in the choroidal tissue. Degenerative and structural changes affecting the choroidea comprise تنكس حصوي (calcific degeneration), تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة (choroidal degeneration), and choroidal sclerosis around the optic disc in both partial (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي) and total (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) forms. Vascular complications recorded as choroidal findings include نزيف مشيمي باثق (persistent choroidal hemorrhage), hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي), and non‑hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين). The choroidea is additionally noted to have a relationship with بُقَعُ روث, although the clinical significance of this association is not detailed. Moreover, the choroidea is described in Arabic as “حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي,” indicating that pathological observations may be localized to a specific genetic chorionic region. Recognizing the choroid as the common locus for these inflammatory, infectious, traumatic, degenerative, vascular, and post‑injury scarring processes—and for its genetic and ancillary associations—is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted imaging, and appropriate therapeutic planning in ophthalmic practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.content: Anxiety (القلق) is characterized by fatigue (التَّعَب) and may present with nonspecific chest pain. Oral candidiasis (داء المبيضات الفموي) is also clinically associated with both nonspecific chest pain and fatigue. The overlap of fatigue and nonspecific chest pain in anxiety and oral candidiasis highlights the need for careful clinical assessment to differentiate between psychological and infectious etiologies, ensuring appropriate diagnosis and management.
